sentences.text: Well, what filtered down to us mostly is that the Jews in Poland had taken out of their homes, that they are being gathered up in ghettos.
We did not realize that they are being taken to annihilation camps.
At least, we kids didn't know because as I mentioned to you, my parents were sort of protective.
But that, too, became more-- more and more information filtered through.
Some were rumors.
Some were true.
It was very difficult to decipher for us kids, the truth from rumor.
Kids don't see things the same way.
However, in 1939 and 1940, we started feeling the pressure even from the Hungarians a little bit.
It was, I believe, in 1940, for example, when laws started coming through.
Jews may not own any stores.
Well, when father came through and told us that he has got to close up the store because the license is not permitted to have a business, well then we knew that it's hitting us.
I mean, there was no more hiding.
Then later on, more orders came in.
Constantly different orders came in from the government, suppressing Jews from doing any business.
And it became tougher and tougher and tougher on us.
We had to close the store and sell the merchandise to a Gentile who opened up smaller stores.
It really was very, very difficult.
So once 1940, '41, my father had to serve first for three months at the time, donating his time to clear the woods and do other work for the Hungarian government.
He was allowed to come home yet, but he had to go back and report again.
The Gentiles were being reported-- had to report to the military.
The Jews had to report to forced labor camp.
So in the beginning, they still allowed him to come home every few months.
And I'm specifically speaking about our territory.
It was different everywhere.
But I can only speak what was happening-- what was happening to us.
And that pressure and those restrictions kept getting stronger and stronger to the point where they denied us to make a living at all.
My father dealt in, for example, in lumber.
He bought large woods, areas of lumber.
And he sold it to people that cut the lumber down.
And that was his sideline.
He made money in that since childhood.
But my mother was running the business, and the maids took care of the kids.
It was more or less unheard of at that time, 50, 60 years ago.
It was unheard of.
But we were modern enough to do that.
But then when my-- they restricted us from doing business, they restricted my father from dealing in lumber, we literally had no way of making a living.
So my father was always innovative.
And what he did, he come worked at the house which had several rooms where the store was, and where they-- what do you call it-- where we'd serve the wine-- it was like a tavern-- and built cages.
And they did allow us to raise angora, angora rabbits.
So we raised angora rabbits.
Now, from where we were to coming down to the stage where we had to raise angora rabbits to make a living, cut the wool and sell the wool, it was quite a come down.
Fortunately, father had some money, and we did not suffer as badly because we had-- we had some money put aside for emergencies.
But it was getting worse and worse, day by day because new orders were coming down.
Evidently, Horthy did not want to allow the Jews to get out of Hungary.
He wanted to use them.

But the pressure from the Germans was constantly on to, to give new orders, and to expedite new restrictions or to enforce new restrictions on Jews and to the point where-- I remember, I believe it was in 1942, when we really knew that there's a problem because we were together for Seder, for the Seder eve, and I remember it so vividly that my father had the Kiddish cup in his hand, and his voice trembled.
And we saw tears coming down his eyes, which we never saw before, because, as I mentioned, he was a very protective person-- protected the kids from the pain of the reality.
But it was obvious in that time that he was scared.
He was terribly scared, and the discussion came after Kiddish of what is really going on.
And we insisted on knowing more, and he said to us at that time that it's not going to get any better.
It's going to get worse, and he's afraid that this may be the last Passover that this family has together.
And he devised a plan that in order for us to survive, we must break up the family.
And the only thing-- the only choice we had, is the three boys must go to different cities, each one.
This way, some of us will survive this catastrophe.
The girls were much too young.
This of course happened after-- also we had a very famous American going through the Carpathian area, lecturing in every town and every village.
His name was Ze'ev Jabotinsky.
He was organizer of the Revisionist movement.
I'll never forget his words when he-- the powerful voice said, Jewish people look up in the sky, and the clouds are black.
They're getting blacker, and thicker, and they'll devour every Jew.
Get out every way you can.
Don't stay here if you value your life.
And if you value your kids, don't stay here.
Well, the words penetrated, and we heard them.
But it wasn't that easy because here we had, as I mentioned my mother had her adopted parents that were in their 80s-- the Hartsteins who adopted my mother.
So my mother asked my father, how can we leave and leave these people alone?
They'll die.
Also, there was-- if something doesn't really hit you over the head, sometimes we think it may never happen.
It's hope.
So we had land that we still got some benefit from it.
We still had a little more freedom than some of the Jewish Polish people that were already-- by 1941, '42 were already taken away from their home into camps.
It didn't happen in Czechoslovakia, I mean, in Carpathia, or in Hungary.
We were actually Hungarian at that time.
Let's speak about Hungary because that time we were Hungarians.
It didn't happen.
We still have freedom of movement, could ride the trains.
We could move from one town to another town, but with restrictions.
So, also my father says, what will I do?
What will-- where will I get started?
I was somebody here.
Where do I get started?
Yet he decided my uncle from this country sent to the family an affidavit to the Czech consulate in Prague.
And my father decided to go down and to try to get a visa for everybody, including my grandparents.
The day he arrived--
To America.
The day he arrived, the consulate closed two days prior to that.
We were shut out.
Had his decision been made possibly two days late-- before, we may have gotten the visa to get into this country.
Because the affidavit was standby in the American consulate for us to be able to get into this country.
But this is what faith is like.
So he missed it by two days.
And that is when he made a decision that each boy will move to a different city.
And I was the one that mother took to Budapest in 19-- towards the end of 1942.
And my brother went to another city called Miskolc.
And my third brother went to-- also to Budapest and then some other places.
But three boys left home, and father and mother was left at home with three-- with the girls.
And my father continued having to report to this forced labor camp.
My mother struggled to the point where I had to send occasionally some bread from Budapest-- loafs of bread, because a loaf of bread meant survival for another week or another day, whatever.
I had to do that as a kid to my parents.
It was that bad.
So from-- coming down from top family in a village to this.
But survival is a very strong motivator.
15. 15-Years-old.
Well, he was-- no.
Jabotinsky's speech was powerful influence.
However, remember that under the Czech government my father was very influential because he had connections in high places-- highly respected in the village.
And he could never, never, never anticipate that it would deteriorate that bad.
Because we-- in Poland, anti-Semitism and the pogroms were going on, and in Russia were going on for a long time.
And the anti-Semitism was so much stronger unless you were the upper echelon.

But in Hungary, it was not that bad-- remote places, occasionally here and there, but in our village where we had those little innuendos, but never, never did he expect that it will come down to this.
No, he didn't.
He did not prepare for it.
But he was a well-informed man, so he must have known about the Nuremberg laws in Germany.
Yes, he probably did, but they did not apply.
He felt that we are protected by the Hungarian government.
He was fluent in Hungarian.
He knew a lot of Hungarian people in Budapest.
And he felt that they will never do to us what Germans do because Hungary was independent.
Hungary was not affected by these Nuremberg, or by the laws by the-- the castle-- I don't know what called anymore.
The castle where they decided the faith of the Jewish people.
The Wannsee conference.
The Wannsee conference-- remember that Germany took over the Russian territory.
They took over Poland.
But they did not conquer Budapest.
You didn't see German soldiers in Hungary.
They had an influence, but you didn't see them-- not at that time, yet.
So the hope was always that somehow the war would be over, and we'll suffer some, but we'll recover.
I wish he had been more scared.
We might have wound up in this country, but I'm not sitting here in judgment.
Goodness knows what any one of us would do at a time like this.
Yes, he was a very intelligent man, but also a man that believed.
He believed.
He had faith in the surroundings and in the people.
And he also had the grandparents that were my wife's-- I mean my mother's parents that he had to deal with, so it was not an easy solution.
I am-- no.
It was very little bit was known.
It was known to some of the hierarchies.
Horthy had a few-- very few-- selected Jewish friends.
But he was not a Jewish-- he was not a lover of the Jews.
He was more-- he considered the Jews as his possession.
I-- no one can look in his heart.
I was much too young.
Now I-- I have to speak in the way I saw it at that time, not what I know.
I know now so much more.
But Horthy did not want to give up his power.
He made an alliance with Germany, and the Jews belonged to him, and you cannot tell me what I should do with them.
Yes, I don't like the Jews either.
I will put them in labor camps, and I will take their business away.
I will cooperate with you, but I will not give them to you because I will use them to-- for forced labor, to whatever, you know, needed to assist military, to carry the munition, [INAUDIBLE].
Whatever there was-- whatever it was needed to do to improve the country for free labor he used the Jews.
And he made it clear that he would not release them.
That was a sore point obviously, with Hitler, that he did not release them.
Definitely not.
Definitely not.
No.
He was known to make very anti-Semitic remark and very derogatory remark.
I mean, he was not that highly cultured of a person.
He was rough person who came into power because it was a soldier [NON-ENGLISH], you know, which is a horse soldier.
So he was not one of intellect.
He was not looked upon as intellectual.
No, I'm convinced that it was not enough loyalty to the Jews, it was mostly out of control.
Was he liked by the Hungarian people?
Liked or loved or--
Yes.
I would say the Gentiles, yes.
The Jews, some in Budapest.
The more elite Jews had access to some of the diplomats, yes.
But the average Jew, the small Jew, [?
Zionist, ?] just, just another anti-Semite.
It's not someone who's going to stand up and risk anything to save us.
Well, in my village I heard them.
And a lots-- and then I heard about them, stories.
Well, some of the stories I've heard that for example, there were some sort of remote villages where the population overran the Hungarian government and the Hungarian soldiers.
And they just beat up-- they did not have a pogrom to the degree where they killed everyone.
But they gathered the Jews.
They beat them.
They burned the shul down.
And Hungarian soldiers just stood by.
Nothing was done about it.
We're talking '39, '40.
So we heard about a story.
But, you know, it was-- we were protected.
We were kids.
So it did not affect us to the point when I was an adult, I relate on a one to one to another human being.
But at that time I was a kid.
I saw that with different eyes as a 14-year-old boy.
I believe it was in 1938.
It was still fairly easy to travel to Hungary for Jews.
You had to have certain permits, but it was not too difficult.
It was actually not so much in our little village.
In our village, everybody went to the next town to-- which is not [INAUDIBLE], but so-- because he couldn't go to every small, little community, but in the next biggest.
Some were totally, totally against him.
You have to realize that most Jews were very, very religious, to the point-- it's like the extreme Hasidim here.
And they believed that Jabotinsky spoke ungodly words.
Why was it ungodly?
Because he spoke of a land, of Palestine, which God is supposed to give us through the coming of messiah.
To be a Zionist was to go against a very traditional concept of, of orthodox Judaism.
It was not popular to be a Zionist.
There were just as many boos as there were people that listened with emotion.
He was not totally accepted because he was a revisionist.
[LAUGHS] One word I have trouble to pronounce.
But he had new concept, new ideas, and most Jewish people were very old fashioned, traditional, same as their parents were 50 years ago, and 100 years ago.
And there were people that did not accept him.
And if the kids organized themselves into a Zionist movement, they were not very popular to kids.
And sometimes we did it.
My parents did not object to it, but some kids had to do it without the permission of their parents.
Because, how dare you promote something which is not in the book.
It's not our time.
Messiah will come.
And that's when the time will be.
I mean, you're going against God's will.
It was a fear, ignorance.
As, you know, when extremists and fanatics have a way of-- I hate to go on tape and say anything to [INAUDIBLE].
It is not my way of doing it.
But I think you understand what I'm not saying.
Up to the time that I had to leave home, yes, in spite of the little-- because we had a great deal of love from our parents.
It was a loving home.
Friday night we sat around the table.
We discussed-- my father openly discussed a lot of cultural things.
He told us background stories from World War I.
He-- my mother was a very well read-- her life was reading.
So whenever my father traveled, all she wanted is to bring her books.
And we were able to read some of those books.
Some, those that were permitted.
It was a happy childhood, yes.
No, I could not.
It was-- a lot of them were Hungarian because my mother read Hungarian.
She did not read Yiddish.
So she read Hungarian books.
So childhood was a happy-- bittersweet childhood is the best I could say it.
Yes, he was in World War I a soldier.
He served in Italy under the Kaiser.
So he referred to all the time to his stories.
No.
Not at that time.
Not that we knew of.
I never heard of anything.
But the stories were very interesting.
They were very fascinating.
So while we lived in a small community, you must be realize, we did not miss television.
We did not miss telephone because we didn't know about it.
It didn't exist.
We did not miss fax machines and all the things that we have.
It didn't exist, and we didn't know that of it's existence.
So the luxuries were that we had a bicycle-- probably one out of two families in the village.
We had a radio that ran on accumulator and a battery, which we had to take in every month to recharge.

But on Sunday mornings, my father put it out in the window, and as the Gentile kids came out of church. or the Gentile people, 100, 200, 300 at a time, sat around the house and my father put on the radio in the window, turned on Budapest station and listen to it.
So we were always get excited because things were always happening around our house.
My father and the priest were intimate friends because they had mutual respect.
My father was a religious, the priest was religious, but my father was modern enough to respect and gain respect.
So there was this, this-- that was the kind of life-- very simple, very humble, small community.
But yes, that was happiness.
Wooden toys and-- but they were happy.
There were happy moments.
I do not have sad memories of my childhood.
I have-- my greatest regret that I was never able to tell my father how wonderful they were.
I've suffered, but that's [INAUDIBLE] because they were good.
They were good parents.
They were loving parents, and I--
I did not have a chance to say thank you.
Because I am, basically, for what I have learned as a child.
What I did is, after I lost my wife, I went through a sort of depression.
Fortunately it was not a deep enough depression where I couldn't function.
It was functional depression for a year or two.
And everything-- so I did seek out help on a few occasions.
And one of the things that was very disturbing to me, because of the loss of my wife, I also suffered again.
I did not suffer the loss of my parents when I was very young because I didn't feel it.
I was a kid, and you handle it differently.
But it was different then.
I was 50 years old.
And I explained it to the psychiatrist that I'm, I'm trying to recover from this depression, but also it haunts me, the memory that I've never been able to write to my father.
So upon her suggestion, I sat down and I wrote him a six page letter, and put it away in my wallet.
And with instructions that it should not be opened up till I-- till I'm gone.
But I expressed myself to my father in writing, and it's still there.
And that was a great, great relief to me.
Is there anything in that letter that you would feel comfortable sharing with us right now?
It's too emotional.
And I become too emotional if I talk about it.
Even my kids don't know that it exists.
They'll know when-- once they watch this tape.
But it exists in the wallet, and they'll find it.
Because my kids never met my parents, I mean, of course not.
No, but it's, it's too emotional, and I would rather pass that.
No, it isn't.
But I do have a-- I do have a long way to go through my Holocaust experiences, which are traumatic.
And I would not want to get myself into such a depressive state because of that that I would not be able to express myself by my feelings without being effected more than necessary.
So I would preserve my strength and my emotions towards that subject.
Does it make sense?
